hero.title: MKS reference for release 0.2 beta
hero.description: Practical syntax, control flow, pointers, namespace imports, package.pkg packages, .mkspkg artifacts, stdlib modules, terminal UI helpers, process capture, runtime behavior, and cookbook examples.
hero.meta: Synced with README, REFERENCE, USER_GUIDE, PACKAGES, std/process, std/tty, std/io, std/path, and std/json

sections.title: Hello, MKS
sections.description: Small first example using variables, stdlib, watch, and output.
sections.source: Checked against README examples and std/math usage.

sections.title: Comments
sections.description: Single-line comments use //.

sections.title: Values & variables
sections.description: Core values are numbers, strings, arrays, objects, modules, functions, booleans, pointers, and null. Variables use var for binding and =: for reassignment.
points: Create variables with var.
Reassign existing variables with =:.
Swap two variables with <-->.
Arrays are indexed with [index].
Object and module fields are accessed with dot syntax.

sections.title: Assignment model & aliasing
sections.description: MKS has value-like scalars and handle-like runtime objects. This matters when you pass values to functions or assign them to another variable.
points: Numbers, booleans, and null behave like copied scalar values.
Strings, arrays, objects, modules, functions, blueprints, and pointers behave like copied handles.
Rebinding a function parameter does not rebind the caller's variable.
Mutating a shared array/object or writing through a pointer is visible through other aliases.

sections.title: null
sections.description: null represents the absence of a value. It is useful for missing fields, failed lookups, and safe fallback returns.
points: The literal is null.
null is falsy in conditions.
null ?= null is true.
null !? null is false.
String(null) produces "null" and Int(null) produces 0.
Avoid arithmetic or ordering comparisons on null unless the docs explicitly define that behavior.

sections.title: Conversions with Int and String
sections.description: Use conversion helpers when you need to turn runtime values into a specific representation.
points: String(value) converts a value to text for display or concatenation.
Int(value) converts supported values to integer form.
Booleans convert to 1 or 0.
null converts to 0 for Int and "null" for String.
Invalid conversions may raise runtime errors.

sections.title: Pointers
sections.description: MKS supports address-of and dereference for variables, array elements, and object fields.
sections.warning: Pointer semantics are useful for low-level experiments, but the language is still experimental. Treat pointer-heavy code carefully.
points: Use &value to take an address.
Use *ptr to read through a pointer.
Use *ptr =: value to write through a pointer.
Taking the address of a temporary expression is rejected.

tabs.label: Python
code: # Python has references, not explicit pointers.

sections.title: Expressions & operators
sections.description: Arithmetic, comparisons, and logic.

sections.title: Expression grouping
sections.description: Parentheses and operator precedence behave as expected.

sections.title: Booleans
sections.description: MKS supports true and false values. std/bool also exposes helper functions.
points: true and false are boolean values.
Booleans can be printed, compared, and used in conditions.
std/bool exposes helpers such as not, and, and or.

sections.title: Write & Writeln
sections.description: Basic built-in output helpers.

sections.title: Read input
sections.description: Read, ReadLine, and ReadWord are native helpers for simple terminal input.
points: Read() and ReadLine() read one line and return it as a string.
Read("prompt") prints the prompt first.
ReadWord() reads the first whitespace-separated word.
Read(0) is a shortcut for first-word behavior.
If input cannot be read, current behavior returns an empty string.

sections.title: if / else
sections.description: Branch with arrow blocks.

sections.title: else if chains
sections.description: Use else if when several branches are mutually exclusive.

sections.title: switch / case / default
sections.description: switch evaluates one value once and runs the first matching case. There is no fallthrough.
points: switch(value) compares the switch value against each case.
The first matching case body runs.
default runs if no case matches.
There is no fallthrough, so break is not needed between cases.

sections.title: while loops
sections.description: State-driven loops.

sections.title: for loops
sections.description: Classic init / condition / step loop header.

sections.title: repeat loops
sections.description: Fixed-count loops, with or without an iterator variable.

sections.title: break & continue
sections.description: Control the nearest loop.

sections.title: Functions
sections.description: Define and call functions with fnc.

sections.title: Early return
sections.description: Return from inside conditionals.

sections.title: Recursion
sections.description: Functions can call themselves. The runtime also has recursion safety limits to prevent runaway calls.
sections.warning: Deep recursion can hit the runtime recursion guard. Prefer loops for huge workloads.

sections.title: Tests with test / expect
sections.description: Executable checks inside MKS source files.

sections.title: Arrays & indexing
sections.description: Create arrays, index into them, mutate elements, and inspect length.

sections.title: Empty arrays
sections.description: Create empty arrays and grow them.

sections.title: Nested arrays
sections.description: Arrays can contain arrays.

sections.title: Core array methods
sections.description: Common built-in array methods used in tests and std/array.

sections.title: Strings
sections.description: Strings are first-class values with built-in methods and std/string wrappers.

sections.title: Stdlib: string
sections.description: std/string wraps native string methods in plain MKS functions.

sections.title: Entities
sections.description: Lightweight object-style values with fields and methods.

tabs.label: MKS
code: Writeln("Hello, I am ", self.name, " with power ", self.power);

tabs.label: Python
code: print("Hello, I am", self.name, "with power", self.power)

sections.title: Extend built-ins
sections.description: Attach new methods to built-in runtime types.
points: Stable extension targets include array, string, and number.
Methods are called with normal dot syntax.
Duplicate method names for the same target are rejected.
Repeated imports of the same module should not re-register the same extension.

sections.title: Imports with using
sections.description: using loads one namespace object. Exported names stay inside that namespace instead of being copied into the current scope.
points: using works with std modules, relative files, and dotted package imports.
A module binds as one namespace value, usually through an alias.
Access exports with dot syntax, for example math.square(4).
Imported modules load declarations and exports. Normal top-level executable code is skipped during import.

sections.title: Packages, package.pkg, and .mkspkg
sections.description: Packages are namespace modules. They let you split MKS code into reusable libraries and install them into other projects.
sections.source: Checked against docs/PACKAGES.md and release 0.2 package behavior.
points: A package is described by package.pkg.
package "name"; defines the import name.
lib "src/lib.mks"; defines the root module loaded by using package.name.
main "src/main.mks"; defines the application entrypoint when present.
mks pkg build creates a .mkspkg artifact.
using imports the package as a namespace object, not as global names.
sections.warning: Legacy mks.toml / packages/ resolution can still exist as compatibility fallback, but new packages should use package.pkg, src/lib.mks, and mks_modules/ or flat .mkspkg installs.

sections.title: Create packages with mks pkg init
sections.description: mks pkg init creates the starter package layout so you do not have to write every file by hand.
points: mks pkg init name creates a new package folder.
The generated package uses package.pkg.
The package root is usually src/lib.mks.
For app packages, add or keep src/main.mks.

sections.title: Building .mkspkg artifacts
sections.description: .mkspkg is the installable package artifact format used by MKS package resolution.
points: mks pkg build reads package.pkg.
It copies package.pkg, source modules, custom manifest paths, and optional README/LICENSE into the archive.
It creates a versioned artifact under dist/.
It creates an install-ready artifact that can be copied into another app.
Package resolution prefers .mkspkg artifacts over unpacked development folders.

sections.title: Flat package installs
sections.description: For small apps, a .mkspkg can live next to the script instead of inside mks_modules.
points: mks_modules/name.mkspkg is the clean package install layout.
Flat install means putting name.mkspkg next to your app script.
Flat install is useful for quick demos and portable folders.
mks_modules is better for larger projects with many dependencies.

sections.title: Package submodules
sections.description: Dotted imports can load submodules inside a package.
points: using cool.lib as lib loads the package root module.
using cool.lib.http.client as client loads a submodule.
Submodules map to files like src/http/client.mks.
Relative imports inside a package archive stay inside that archive.

sections.title: .mkspkg archive format
sections.description: The package artifact is currently a simple uncompressed archive.
points: The archive magic header is MKSPKG1\n.
Each entry stores path length, content size, path bytes, and content bytes.
A zero path length terminates the archive.
There is no compression yet.
This simple format keeps loading easy and predictable.

sections.title: Stdlib: math
sections.description: Small math helpers and wrappers.

sections.title: Stdlib: array
sections.description: Plain-MKS helper functions built on array methods and loops.

tabs.label: MKS
code: fnc add(acc, x) -> return acc + x; <-

tabs.label: Python
code: print(reduce(lambda acc, x: acc + x, xs, 0))

sections.title: Stdlib: time
sections.description: Simple wrappers for current time and sleeping.

sections.title: Stdlib: io
sections.description: Stream-based input and output helpers for stdin, stdout, and stderr.
points: Use io.stdout for normal output.
Use io.stderr for errors, warnings, and diagnostics.
Use io.write(stream, ...) to write without a newline.
Use io.writeln(stream, ...) to write with a newline.
Use io.flush(stream) to force buffered output to appear.
Use io.read_line(prompt) to read a full line.
Use io.read_word(prompt) to read only the first word.

tabs.label: MKS
tabs.note: This is more explicit than Writeln because the first argument chooses the stream.

sections.title: Stdlib: process
sections.description: Run external programs, read CLI arguments, get the current working directory, and capture command output.
sections.source: Checked against std/process.c and release 0.2 beta behavior.
points: Use process.cwd() to get the current working directory.
Use process.args() or process.argv() to get CLI arguments.
Use process.exec(cmd, args) when you only need the exit code.
Use process.shell(command) when you want to run a shell command and only need the exit code.
Use process.capture(cmd, args) when you need stdout and stderr as strings.
Use process.capture_shell(command) for shell pipelines like ls -la | head.
Use process.exit(code) to abort the current MKS run with an exit code.
sections.warning: Shell helpers run through /bin/sh -c. Do not pass untrusted user input directly into process.shell or process.capture_shell.

tabs.label: MKS
tabs.note: process.exec starts a program directly with an argv array. process.capture does the same, but returns an object with code, out, and err.

sections.title: Process capture
sections.description: Capture helpers let MKS run a command and keep its output inside the program instead of printing directly to the terminal.
points: The return value is an object with code, out, and err.
code is the process exit code. Usually 0 means success.
out is stdout.
err is stderr.
Capture is the right choice for TUI apps, because raw command output can destroy the terminal layout.
sections.warning: Captured output can contain newlines. Draw it with a multiline text widget or split it before rendering in a TUI.

sections.title: Process exit
sections.description: process.exit stops the current MKS program with an exit code.
points: process.exit() exits with code 0.
process.exit(code) exits with the provided code.
Use nonzero codes for failures in scripts and CI tasks.

sections.title: Stdlib: tty
sections.description: Low-level terminal control for building TUI apps: cursor movement, raw keyboard input, alternate screen, colors, boxes, windows, buttons, and text drawing.
points: Use tty.alt_screen() to draw on a temporary terminal screen.
Use tty.raw() to read keys instantly without waiting for Enter.
Use tty.clear() to clear the screen.
Use tty.move(x, y) or tty.write_at(x, y, text) for positioned output.
Use tty.window, tty.box, tty.button, and tty.input_box as widget primitives.
Always call tty.restore() before exiting.
sections.warning: If a program crashes after raw mode or cursor hiding, the terminal can look broken. Run reset in the shell or make sure tty.restore() is called.

tabs.label: MKS
tabs.note: tty is intentionally low-level. A package like ggui can build panels, lists, inputs, dialogs, and layouts on top of it.

sections.title: Keyboard input with tty
sections.description: Raw mode lets MKS read keyboard events directly, which is required for interactive terminal apps.
points: tty.read_key() waits for a key and returns a string.
tty.read_key_timeout(ms) waits up to the timeout and can return null/no key.
Arrow keys return names like up, down, left, and right.
Enter returns enter.
Backspace returns backspace.
Ctrl-C returns ctrl_c instead of killing the process when raw mode is active.

tabs.label: Blocking
code: tty.write_at(2, 4, "press Esc or Ctrl-C to exit");

tabs.label: Timeout
tabs.note: Timeout input is useful for animations, clocks, spinners, and event loops that should keep drawing even when the user does not press a key.

sections.title: Terminal size and layout fallbacks
sections.description: TTY apps need terminal dimensions. size_or is useful when the program runs outside a real terminal or inside CI.
points: tty.size() returns an object with w and h.
tty.size_or(w, h) returns terminal size or fallback dimensions.
Use this when drawing demos, tests, or CI-safe output.

sections.title: Wrapping text in terminal UIs
sections.description: Use text wrapping for multiline content such as command output, logs, help text, and error messages.
points: Single-line text helpers should not receive large multiline strings.
Captured process output often contains newlines.
tty.text_wrap is designed for drawing text inside a rectangular area.
This prevents command output from destroying your box/window layout.
sections.warning: Do not draw multiline command output through button/input/single-line text helpers. Use text_wrap or split it into lines.

sections.title: Stdlib: path
sections.description: Helpers for building and inspecting file paths without manually slicing strings.
points: Use path.join to combine path parts.
Use path.basename to get the final file or directory name.
Use path.dirname to get the parent path.
Use path.extname to get the extension.
Use path.stem to get the file name without extension.
Use path.normalize to clean a path string.

sections.title: Stdlib: json
sections.description: Parse JSON text into MKS values and stringify MKS values back into JSON.
points: Use json.parse(text) to convert JSON text into MKS values.
JSON arrays become MKS arrays.
JSON objects become MKS objects.
JSON strings become MKS strings.
JSON null becomes MKS null.
Use json.stringify(value) to convert MKS values back into JSON text.
sections.warning: Unicode escape support such as \uXXXX may be limited in the current preview. Prefer plain UTF-8 strings when possible.

sections.title: watch / on change
sections.description: Watch a named variable and run code when it changes.
points: watch registers interest in a variable name.
on change runs after that variable is updated.
Handlers are synchronous.
Deep object/array mutation and pointer-mediated writes are not the same as direct variable reassignment.
sections.warning: Do not treat watch as a full reactive deep-state system yet. It currently tracks named variable changes, not every nested mutation.

sections.title: defer
sections.description: Register cleanup-like work to run when leaving the scope.

sections.title: MKS command line
sections.description: The mks executable can run scripts, start a REPL, show version information, and build packages.
points: mks file.mks runs a script.
mks --repl starts interactive mode when available.
mks --version prints the installed version.
mks pkg init and mks pkg build manage packages.
Installed standard library files may be found through install paths or MKS_STD_PATH depending on your setup.

sections.title: Performance notes
sections.description: MKS is experimental, so performance guidance is mostly about avoiding avoidable interpreter overhead.
points: Prefer loops over extremely deep recursion.
Avoid repeatedly creating unnecessary temporary arrays/strings in hot loops.
Keep package and stdlib imports at module top level.
Use native stdlib helpers when they exist.
Benchmark release builds, not sanitizer/debug builds.

sections.title: Errors
sections.description: MKS reports syntax, runtime, and module-resolution errors with file, line, column, help text, and examples.
tips: Check typos in keywords and names.
Check package names and mks_modules/ when using packages.

sections.title: Cookbook: sum numbers
sections.description: Classic accumulator loop.

sections.title: Cookbook: safe last item
sections.description: Return null for empty arrays.

sections.title: Cookbook: custom array method
sections.description: Attach a reusable method with extend array.

sections.title: Cookbook: mutable entity state
sections.description: Entity methods can update self fields.

sections.title: Cookbook: split code into a module
sections.description: Move reusable helpers into another .mks file.

sections.title: Cookbook: create a tiny package
sections.description: Create, build, and use an installable .mkspkg library.

sections.title: Cookbook: capture command output
sections.description: Run a system command and keep stdout/stderr inside MKS.

sections.title: Cookbook: terminal window
sections.description: Draw a small terminal UI window and wait for a key.

sections.title: Cookbook: draw command output in a TUI
sections.description: Capture command output and draw it inside a terminal window without breaking the layout.

sections.title: Cookbook: watched counter
sections.description: Print whenever a value changes.

sections.title: Cookbook: prompt for a name
sections.description: Tiny interactive script using Read.

sections.title: Cookbook: tiny test suite
sections.description: Keep examples executable as checks.
